coredata.dc:title: Visible-light excited carbon dots with dual emission long-afterglow for temperature sensing and anti-counterfeiting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Feng
Sun, Xiangying
coredata.dc:description: Long-afterglow luminescent materials has undergone extensive research in sensing and anti-counterfeiting. However, the complex preparation process, single luminescence with low quantum yields, potential biological toxicity and harsh application conditions limited the widespread use of these materials. In this work, 3-APBA CDs@MA was obtained by 3-aminophenylboronic acid, sulfuric acid and melamine via a one-pot hydrothermal process. The as-designed 3-APBA CDs@MA exhibit dual emission with quantum yields of 15.74 % and lifetime of 1.39 s for thermally activated delayed fluorescence and 1.63 s for room temperature phosphorescence. The afterglow of carbon dots can be observed by the naked eye for about 24 s and 13 s after turning off the 365 nm UV lamp and visible light lamp, respectively. Furthermore, the as-obtained 3-APBA CDs@MA show potential for temperature sensing and advanced anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Visible light-excited
Dual emission
Temperature sensing
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of 3-APBA CDs@MA

The authors also thank the Instrumental Analysis Center of Huaqiao University for analysis support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of 3-APBA CDs (a) TEM image; (b) HRTEM image; (c) XRD of 3-APBA CDs; (d) AFM image.
(a) FTIR spectra of 3-APBA CDs@MA; (b) XPS survey scan of 3-APBA CDs@MA; (c)C 1s, (d)O 1s, (e)N 1s, (f) S 2p, (g)B 1s XPS spectra of 3-APBA CDs@MA.

(a) UV–vis spectra of 3-APBA CDs@MA, MA CDs, 3-APBA CDs; (b) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of 3-APBA CDs@MA; (c) Afterglow decay curves of 3-APBA CDs@MA; (d) 3-APBA CDs@MA afterglow digital photos under UV lamp on/off and visible light lamp on/off.
Effect of (a) inorganic acid and (b) carbon source on 3-APBA CDs@MA; (c) Spectra of the luminescence mechanism of 3-APBA CDs@MA; (d) Afterglow spectra of 3-APBA CDs@MA and 3-APBA CDs + MA.

(a) Temperature variation of afterglow emission of 3-APBA CDs@MA; (b) Linear graph of afterglow emission of 3-APBA CDs@MA with temperature; (c) Photographs of 3-APBA CDs@MA with different temperature.
Illustration for the preparation and the application of 3-APBA CDs@MA
The lifetime test results of TADF and RTP.

head.ce:title: Visible-light excited carbon dots with dual emission long-afterglow for temperature sensing and anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Huaqiao University, Key Laboratory of Molecular Designing and Green Conversions in Fujian, Xiamen, 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Key Laboratory of Molecular Designing and Green Conversions in Fujian

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Huaqiao University, Key Laboratory of Molecular Designing and Green Conversions in Fujian, Xiamen, 361021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Long-afterglow luminescent materials has undergone extensive research in sensing and anti-counterfeiting. However, the complex preparation process, single luminescence with low quantum yields, potential biological toxicity and harsh application conditions limited the widespread use of these materials. In this work, 3-APBA CDs@MA was obtained by 3-aminophenylboronic acid, sulfuric acid and melamine via a one-pot hydrothermal process. The as-designed 3-APBA CDs@MA exhibit dual emission with quantum yields of 15.74 % and lifetime of 1.39 s for thermally activated delayed fluorescence and 1.63 s for room temperature phosphorescence. The afterglow of carbon dots can be observed by the naked eye for about 24 s and 13 s after turning off the 365 nm UV lamp and visible light lamp, respectively. Furthermore, the as-obtained 3-APBA CDs@MA show potential for temperature sensing and advanced anti-counterfeiting.

Visible light-excited carbon dots with dual emission long-afterglow were synthesized by 3-aminophenylboronic acid, sulfuric acid and melamine via one-pot hydrothermal method. The as-designed 3-APBA CDs@MA exhibits dual emission with room temperature phosphorescence and thermal activated delayed fluorescence, 15.74 % quantum yields, 1.39 s and 1.63 s lifetime (TADF and RTP, respectively).Then the as-obtained 3-APBA CDs@MA was applied in anti-counterfeiting and temperature sensing.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Long-afterglow luminescent materials [1] has taken wide attention in the aspects of biological imaging [2], anti-counterfeiting [3], fingerprinting [4], sensing [5] and advanced dynamic information encryption [6] due to their unique properties. Until now, the preparation of afterglow luminescent materials has been primarily achieved via rare earth metal ions forming complex [7], polymer engineering [8,9], covalent bonding interaction [10,11] and host-guest interaction [12,13]. However, high manufacturing cost, potential biological toxicity and complex preparation processes have limited the widespread synthesis and application of these materials [14]. Therefore, there is an urgent need for convenient strategies and environmentally friendly materials to overcome the above shortcomings (see Scheme 1 ).

Carbon dots (CDs) as a type of zero-dimensional nanomaterials have various nanostructures with diameters less than 10 nm. CDs are usually doped with heteroatoms, making them hydrophilic and promoting various surface functional groups [15–17] modifications or passivation, resulting in a series of advantages such as photoluminescence, electroluminescence, catalysis, low toxicity and excellent water solubility [18,19]. By restraining the intermolecular vibration and solving the problem of the stability of the triplet excited state in CDs, room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) can be achieved [20]. At the same time, the introduction of heteroatoms/heavy metals in the synthesis of CDs can increase the spin orbital coupling (SOC) constant [21], promoting the intersystem crossing (ISC) from the lowest singlet state S1 to the triplet state Tn, and achieving enhanced RTP [22]. Additionally, with the realization of the reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) in CDs, the energy changes from the triplet state T1 to the lowest single state S1 and then returns to the ground state S0, delayed fluorescence can be achieved [23,24]. Meanwhile, CDs with dual-emission afterglow signal have the various advantages like high sensitivity, accuracy [25], visualization [26] and good anti-interference, but this type of CDs are rarely reported. Shen group [22] used boric acid as the matrix, dual emission afterglow CDs were obtained. Lin group [27] developed a method to achieve visible light and near infrared dual-mode afterglow emissions from CDs-based materials at ambient conditions. Liu group [28] reported the temperature-responsive conversion characteristics of thermal activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) and RTP of CDs in silica (CDs@SiO2) for the first time, and applied in rapid fingerprint detection and temperature sensing. Furthermore, afterglow CDs that can be excited by visible light have less harm to people and environment, more flexible application and lower cost. Therefore, it is also an urgent need to develop a new type of CDs that can be excited by visible light with high quantum yields [29], dual emission afterglow and could maintain afterglow underwater.

In this work, we synthesized a new type of CDs by 3-aminophenylboronic acid, sulfuric acid and melamine (3-APBA CDs@MA) via one pot hydrothermal method. The as-obtained 3-APBA CDs@MA embedded in melamine can be excited by ultraviolet or visible light with dual emission long-afterglow. With various heteroatoms dopped in 3-APBA CDs@MA promoted n-π* transition of energy, the triplet state of 3-APBA CDs@MA was effectively filled. The as-obtained 3-APBA CDs@MA showed TADF lifetime of 1.39 s, RTP lifetime of 1.63 s and quantum yields of 15.74 %. Interestingly, the afterglow would not be quenched when 3-APBA CDs@MA was dispersed in water, methyl alcohol, ethyl alcohol, acetone, and acetonitrile. Therefore, this work proposes a new one-step hydrothermal method for manufacturing long-afterglow luminescent materials. Finally, 3-APBA CDs@MA was applied in temperature sensing and advanced anti-counterfeiting.

3-aminophenylboronic acid (3-APBA), melamine (MA), henylboric acid (PBA), 4-formylphenyl-boric acid (4-FPBA), 4-carboxyphenylboric acid (4-CPBA) and 4-hydroxyphenylboric acid (4-HPBA) were purchased from Innochem. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), nitric acid (HNO3), methyl alcohol (MeOH), ethyl alcohol (EtOH), acetonitrile (ACN) and acetone (ACE) were purchased from Sinopharm. Deionized (DI) water was used in the experiments. All reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial sources and used directly without further purification.

All types of 3-APBA CDs@MA was synthesized by hydrothermal method (conditions optimization and afterglow spectra refer to supporting information 1.1 and Fig. S1). The processes were described in the best synthesis conditions as an example. In short, 0.1g of 3-APBA and 0.6 g of MA were added into 4.0 mL of DI water then added 20 μL of H2SO4, transferred the mixture into a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 6 h. The solid powder 3-APBA CDs@MA was obtained after filtrated to obtain filter cake, added 30 mL DI water and stirred for 10 min. After doing this for three times, the dialysis bag of 500 Da was used for 24 h. Finally, dried as-obtained 3-APBA CDs@MA in oven at 60 °C for 6 h and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. Afterglow of 3-APBA CDs@MA before and after purification showed in Fig. S2.

All the temperatures were held at the corresponding temperature for 60 s and then measured afterglow emission intensity. After excited by 365 nm UV light and visible light lamp, phosphorescent images were recorded with the mobile phone.

UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using Agilent Technologies Cary 5000. Steady-state photoluminescence/phosphorescence spectra were measured using Hitachi F-7000. Fluorescence lifetimes were obtained by FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were taken by Thermo Scientific FEI/Talos F200X G2 (Enfinium SE 976). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded by MiniFlex600 (Rigaku, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained from the Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS) were recorded on a K-alpha + spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Atomic force microscope (AFM) image was taken by MultiMode8 (Bruker, USA). Digital photos were taken by iPhone 11 under the irradiation of a hand-held UV lamp at room temperature. Ultrapure water was prepared by a Milli-Q water purification system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of 3-APBA CDs@MA
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological structure of 3-APBA CDs@MA was characterized by TEM, XRD and AFM. TEM image showed that 3-APBA CDs (3-APBA CDs@MA without MA) had a relatively uniform particle size of about 2.6 nm (inside illustration). The HRTEM showed the lattice spacing of 3-APBA CDs was 0.21 nm (Fig. 1a and 1b). As shown XRD spectra, there were still obvious MA diffraction peaks in 3-APBA CDs@MA. As-obtained 3-APBA CDs@MA without MA(3-APBA CDs) had a new weak diffraction peaks at 2θ = 23.48°, which correspond to the diffraction peaks of graphitic carbon (002) in Fig. 1c [30,31]. In XRD spectra of MA and 3-APBA CDs@MA, MA diffraction peaks appeared at 2θ = 26.34° and 2θ = 28.92° correspond to characteristic melamine (12) and (112) in Fig. S3. After the synthesis process, the MA lattice structure was still obvious but the (12) diffraction peaks intensity decreased and (112) diffraction peaks intensity increased. That may prove MA had polymerized and graphitized [32]. AFM image (Fig. 1d) suggested that 3-APBA CDs@MA was spherical or quasi-spherical structures [33] and heights consistent with their average particle sizes in TEM characterization.

In 3-APBA CDs@MA FTIR spectra (Fig. 2a), the absorption peaks at 1179 cm−1 and 1089 cm−1 should be attributed to the stretching vibrations of the S–O and C–S group [34] (see Fig. 1). The absorption peak at 1376 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibrations of B–O group. In addition, the absorption peak at 1540 cm−1 should be attributed to CN group stretching vibrations, implying the production of more sp2 structures in 3-APBA CDs@MA [4,35].

The chemical composition of 3-APBA CDs@MA was further characterized by XPS. The results showed that 3-APBA CDs@MA mainly contain C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, S 2p and B 1s, whose corresponding binding energies were 286.7 eV, 530.1 eV, 398.5 eV, 167.6 eV and 190.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 2b). The high-resolution C 1s spectra indicated three diverse chemical environments, including CN (287.8 eV), graphitic sp2 C (284.9 eV) [36] and C–B (284.1 eV) (Fig. 2c). The high-resolution O 1s spectrum contained two peaks at hydroxyl group (-OH) (532.0 eV) and -O-S (530.7 eV) (Fig. 2d). The high-resolution N 1s spectrum was differentiated into two peaks at C–NH–C (399.2 eV) and NC (398.3 eV) (Fig. 2e); the high-resolution S 2p spectra can be divided into two peaks C–S(O)x-C group (168.9 eV) [33] and sulfonate group (-SO3H) (167.4 eV) (Fig. 2f). The high-resolution B 1s spectra were comprised of two peaks at BCO2 (191.8 eV), B–N and/or B–C bonds in planar BC3 nanodomains (190.7 eV) [31,37,38] (Fig. 2g). The XPS results of B 1s and C 1s revealed that 3-APBA and MA were bound by B–N bond successfully.

UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 3 a showed an absorption band in the range of 345 nm–490 nm that was mainly attributed to n-π* transition of CN group could be found in 3-APBA CDs@MA and 3-APBA CDs indicated that 3-APBA CDs may embedded with MA. Moreover, the absorption band at 253 nm was attributed to π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains in carbon core [39,40]. The luminescence spectra of 3-APBA CDs@MA in Fig. 3b showed fluorescence emission peak and TADF peak appeared at 435 nm and RTP peak appeared at 505 nm, respectively. Under different excitation wavelengths, afterglow of 3-APBA CDs@MA showed clearly stimulation dependency and the optimal excitation wavelength was 385 nm (Fig. S4a). The afterglow decay curves of 3-APBA CDs@MA was showed in Fig. 3c and the afterglow lifetime of RTP and TADF were measured as 1.63 s and 1.39 s, respectively. Test conditions were shown in SI and the lifetime test results were listed in Table 1 , and all these attenuation spectra conform to the triple exponential function. Based on the following eq. (1):
(1)
τ
a v g
= ∑
B i
τ i 2
/
B i
τ i
Where τ i is the life components in bi-exponential decay model; B i is constants.

Additionally, 3-APBA CDs@MA exhibited good resistance to photobleaching (Fig. S4b) and good stability in air (Fig. S4c) water, MeOH, EtOH, ACN and ACE due to the poor solubility and strong stability of MA (Fig. S5). The afterglow of 0.523 g 3-APBA CDs@MA could still be observed for 24 s and 13 s by the naked eye after being excited for 60 s at room temperature by a 365 nm UV lamp and visible light lamp, respectively (Fig. 3d and videos in Fig. S6a and S6b). The influence of excitation time on the afterglow was also explored, after the 3-APBA CDs@MA was excited by UV light for 10 s, 30 s, 60 s, 120 s and 180 s, the afterglow time remained basically unchanged (Fig. S7).

The following experiments were used to demonstrate the role of each material in the afterglow. Firstly, we synthesized 3-APBA CDs and MA CDs using the above strategy and the phosphorescence spectra were shown in Fig. S8a. The UV–vis absorption of these CDs in Fig. S8b showed only 3-APBA CDs and 3-APBA CDs@MA had n-π* transition of CN. Secondly, other inorganic acids including HCl and HNO3 substituted H2SO4 to synthesize 3-APBA CDs@MA. The afterglow and UV–vis spectra of these CDs were shown in Fig. 4 a. Only CDs synthesized with H2SO4 can exhibit the strongest dual afterglow emission (Compared XPS characterization of 3-APBA CDs@MA before and after purification shown in Fig. S9). Finally, we replaced 3-APBA with PBA, 4-FPBA, 4-CPBA and 4-HPBA to synthesize CDs under the same synthesis conditions and afterglow emission spectra of these CDs were showed in Fig. 4b, only 3-APBA CDs@MA exhibiting the strong afterglow emission. These results revealed that sulfuric acid participated in heteroatomic doping to form n-π* conjugate system and the S element doping could effectively reduce the energy gap (ΔEST), promoting the intersystem crossing from the lowest singlet state (S1) to the triplet state (T1) [41]. Meanwhile, the covalent bonds promoted the RISC from the triplet state (T1) to the lowest single state (S1) (Fig. 4c). Interestingly, as the ability of substituents to activate benzene decreased, the afterglow emission of these CDs gradually weakened. In addition, 3-APBA CDs@MA was obtained by a second hydrothermal synthesis of 3-APBA CDs and MA and afterglow emission spectra revealed that the afterglow properties of these two CDs were almost identical (Fig. 4d). In short, the degree of carbonization, doping of sulfur elements, –NH2 in the benzene of 3-APBA played a crucial role in the formation the RTP and TADF of 3-APBA CDs@MA.

Compared to single-emission signal afterglow in temperature sensing, dual-emission signals were more sensitive and enabling visual sensing. In this work, due to the increase TADF emission intensity but decrease RTP emission intensity with increasing temperature, the as-obtained 3-APBA CDs@MA have been applied in visual temperature sensing. As afterglow spectrum showed in Fig. 5 a, the functional relation of afterglow emission intensity with temperature changed from 20 °C to 80 °C as described in eq. (2).
(2) Int (I1 / I2) = 0.041 T - 0.580

its correlation coefficient is 0.987, where T represents the temperature (oC), and I1, I2 represents the intensity of TADF and RTP, respectively. The linear graph of 3-APBA CDs@MA afterglow change was shown in Fig. 5b. As temperature increased from 30 °C to 80 °C, the 3-APBA CDs@MA afterglow color turned cyan into blue, observable by the naked eye (Fig. 5c).

The strategy for anti-counterfeiting was investigated. In short, a series of CDs were synthesized under pH = 5.16 (3-APBA CDs@MA), pH = 7.28 (3-APBA CDs@MA-I) and pH = 8.36 (3-APBA CDs@MA-II), respectively (Details of the synthesis conditions see SI and Fig. S10). Then, these three CDs were used to make up patterns “H Q U” (0.569 g of 3-APBA CDs@MA, 0.638 g of 3-APBA CDs@MA-I and 0.481 g of 3-APBA CDs@MA-II). All patterns can be observed under the 365 nm UV lamp and visible lamp. However, after UV lamp was turned off, “U”, “Q”, “H” extinguished sequentially, only “H” could be seen by the naked eye after 3 s. After visible lamp was turned off, only “H” was observed by naked eye after 2 s. This proved that this kind of CDs had excellent anti-counterfeiting. Additional, when the temperature changed from 25 °C to 45 °C and 65 °C, the TADF emission of these three CDs were enhanced and the pattern appeared blue afterglow (Fig. 6 and Fig. S11).

In summary, 3-APBA CDs@MA with high quantum yields, long lifetime and dual-emission long-afterglow synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal process. The as-obtained 3-APBA CDs@MA have excellent luminous properties due to multi-element doping and strong rigid structure. Additionally, 3-APBA CDs@MA showed excellent stability in air and various liquids. The afterglow emission could be observed for 24 s and 13 s by the naked eye at room temperature with UV lamp and visible light lamp turned off, respectively. Due to the opposite signals to temperature, 3-APBA CDs@MA can be used as a temperature-sensitive probe. Furthermore, considering 3-APBA CDs@MA afterglow will not quench under water, these properties make 3-APBA CDs@MA have ability to be further processed and potentially beneficial for anti-counterfeiting or encryption in a humid environment.

Feng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiangying Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Foundation of Graphene Powder & Composite Research Centre of Fujian Province (No. 2017H2001) and Pilot Project of Fujian Province (No. 2021H0017). The authors also thank the Instrumental Analysis Center of Huaqiao University for analysis support.
